campaign: Assume That I Can, So Maybe I Will
one_liner: Assume that I can, and maybe I will.

Assume that I can, and maybe I will.
Mechanisms: self-fulfilling-prophecy, purpose-led-emotion, calendar-moment-hijack, hashtag-participation
Idea: Ahead of World Down Syndrome Day, launch the spot 'Assume That I Can' as a call for an end to prejudice. To break the stereotypes even further, share examples of prejudices that people with Down syndrome have shattered via our social media channels — and invite the community to join in by using the official hashtag #AssumeThatICan.

inferred.insight: Low expectations are not inert — they actively shape what people with Down syndrome are allowed to attempt. Flipping the assumption flips the outcome.
inferred.one_liner: Assume that I can, and maybe I will.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A Down-syndrome advocacy film that reframed expectations as self-fulfilling prophecies, racking up 150M views in one week and global coverage from CBS, BBC, CNN, Vogue and 100+ outlets.

visual.style_description: Cinematic dark-blue ensemble portrait: ten people with Down syndrome (and a few allies) stand in a defiant lineup looking straight to camera, with the campaign mark 'ASSUME THAT I CAN SO MAYBE I WILL' in distressed yellow stencil type stamped across them. A row of vertical poster thumbnails repeating the line in different translations sits at the bottom, anchored by a long press-logo strip.

idea.rationale: Reframing low expectations as self-fulfilling prophecies — 'assume that I can, so maybe I will' — turns the disability-advocacy argument into a one-line attitudinal flip. The idea is rhetorically tight and globally translatable but sits inside a well-populated category (purpose-led emotion films for World Down Syndrome Day), so the originality is in the framing rather than the form.

strategy.rationale: CoorDown is the Italian National Coordination of Associations of People with Down Syndrome, an advocacy body whose annual World Down Syndrome Day campaigns are its core mission, so a film attacking the prejudice mechanism behind limited opportunity is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight that low expectations actively shape outcomes is observationally credible but argued from advocacy first principles rather than from cited research on the board.

execution.rationale: A single World Down Syndrome Day hero film is amplified through CoorDown's social channels with the #AssumeThatICan hashtag and translated posters carrying the line across markets. The orchestration is competent calendar-moment activation but the case board reads as poster-style — a single hero still plus stencil-typography campaign mark plus press-logo strip — without storyboard or keyframe evidence of the film's actual craft.

impact.rationale: 150M views in one week is a high reach number for an advocacy film but is entrant-reported and unaudited, with no view-platform breakdown disclosed. The press-logo strip of CBS, BBC, CNN, Vogue, Forbes, MSNBC and 10+ other international outlets is a credible signal of earned-conversation breadth, though no attitudinal-shift, policy or behavioural-change measure is shown — surrogate metrics for an advocacy brief.

why_it_won.verdict: Earns Bronze because the self-fulfilling-prophecy framing and earned global press are strong, but the board shows almost no film-craft evidence and the impact case rests on unaudited reach.

impact.rationale: Impact is carried almost entirely by trade and culture press — Campaign, Adweek and It's Nice That framing the work as a 'public artwork capturing modern Britain' — which is a credible signal of critical reappraisal of the brand identity. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because it elevates the broadcaster's ident system from station furniture into an anthology of authored short films that critics read as a public artwork of modern Britain.

inferred.qualitative_summary: A concept car retrofitted with absurd 'safety features' for Black drivers made the inequality of US traffic stops physically visible at the Detroit Auto Show, generating broad earned media.

visual.style_description: Dark, cinematic hero shot of a silver four-door sedan parked in deep shadow with its rear-passenger door wide open, flanked by four green-lit feature callouts (steering wheel, removable glove box, removable trunk, transparent doors); bold 'DRIVING WHILE BLACK' title on top and a press strip with the Detroit Auto Show installation underneath.

idea.rationale: A purpose-built concept car whose 'safety features' (transparent doors, removable trunk, removable glove box, dual steering wheel) are the absurd accommodations a Black driver supposedly needs to survive a routine traffic stop is a sharp product-as-protest gesture. But the film axis specifically — the case board reads as a stunt/PR submission rebadged as Film, with the on-board evidence weighted toward the car installation and the auto-show takeover rather than original cinematography.

strategy.rationale: Courageous Conversation Global Foundation is a US non-profit founded by Glenn Singleton to facilitate cross-racial dialogue on systemic inequity, so confronting policing inequity is dead-centre mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context is data-anchored — 'Black drivers are 3x more likely to be killed during an encounter with police' and '600+ Black Americans murdered during routine traffic stops since 2017' — a specific, sourceable claim that gives the rationale jury-credibility.

execution.rationale: The board's execution stack is auto-show launch (Detroit, September 14, 2023), donated-media OOH near Ford Field, branded T-shirts, QR-to-AR experience and website — channels appropriate for a stunt-PR play but light on film evidence. The Film category native-check is weakly satisfied: only one storyboard-style installation image and no keyframes from an actual film artefact appear on the board, which caps how much film craft the jury could reward.

impact.rationale: The board carries no quantified outcomes — no view counts, no policy-conversation lift, no signature totals, no measured shift in awareness — only two press quotes (Adweek and a French Creapills note). For a foundation whose business objective is sustained advocacy traction, the absence of any audience or behaviour metric is the dominant impact-case weakness.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the concept-car-as-protest idea is genuinely strong and the data on policing inequity is precise, but the entry sits awkwardly in Film with thin on-board film evidence and no quantified outcome.

The 'winter doesn't kill the craving, it just hides the sun' insight is observable and consistent with Magnum's pleasure-seeking platform, even if the cultural framing is generic.

execution.rationale: Native-check failure: the board shows no narrative film, no keyframes, no storyboard — only a single billboard rendering and phone-mockup heat-maps. The real craft sits in the data pipeline (sun-tracking algorithm, beacon push notifications, geo-targeted discounts to nearest shop), which is innovation OOH craft rather than film craft. There is no filmic orchestration to evaluate at the level a Film jury rewards.

impact.rationale: +66.3% sales versus the average winter season is the right business outcome for an off-season volume campaign — sales not impressions — though 'average winter' as the baseline is unspecified (which years, which markets) and the absolute volume lift behind the percentage is not disclosed, so '66.3% of what?' remains open. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the seasonality-flip mechanic and sales lift are real, but the work is essentially a data-driven OOH idea submitted into Film with no film artefact for the jury to actually reward.

It's a smaller, quieter idea than Apple's biggest holiday spots, but the song-as-idea is original within the brand's holiday-film canon.

strategy.rationale: Apple is the world's largest consumer-tech brand whose holiday films consistently frame creativity and human connection as the product benefit, so a film arguing 'we're all the same' through a Beatles meditation on human discord is exact mission-fit. The stated insight — that the right song moves emotion more than picture alone — is plausible for Apple's storytelling-tool positioning, though it's argued from craft rather than from external data; partnering with the Harrison estate lends real veracity rather than borrowed reference.

execution.rationale: The craft layer is the music-clearance pipeline itself: an extensive search of thousands of tracks, original demos with nine artists, then two licensed recordings (a bespoke soundtrack version and the original) cleared through Olivia Harrison and the estate. impact.rationale: The single disclosed number — 1 million organic views — is modest by Apple holiday-film standards and an audience metric rather than a business outcome (no iPhone/Mac sales lift, gift-mode purchase, or brand-tracker shift is shown). why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the music licensing itself is the idea — a Harrison-estate-blessed Beatles B-side becomes the emotional spine of an Apple holiday film about empathy.

strategy.rationale: Charoen Pokphand (CP) is one of Asia's largest food conglomerates and a world-leading poultry producer based in Thailand, whose mass-market chicken brand needs to differentiate when every competitor claims the same safety approvals. The insight — that NASA's Space Food Safety Standard is the only credible higher bar above the industry baseline — is real, verifiable category context, and the campaign earns the claim through the actual ISS delivery rather than asserting it.

execution.rationale: Execution leans heavily on the SpaceX/Axiom partnership and the earned-media engine — 108 publishers, TV news, influencer push, OOH and POP — with the film itself acting as the launch artefact. Craft on the board is rated medium, with a poster-style ISS-against-stars layout rather than storyboard or keyframe evidence of the film's cinematography or direction.

impact.rationale: The 4 billion baht incremental sales figure is the right business outcome for a mass-market food brand and is reported in absolute terms rather than as an unanchored percentage, which is genuinely strong. The 1.3 billion impressions and No.1 Thailand / No.3 worldwide Twitter trending are entrant-reported claims rather than audited reach, and the '2x higher ad recall than an average' is unanchored — 'average of what cohort?' — so the headline sales number carries most of the credible Impact weight.

why_it_won.verdict: Earns Bronze because the literal-aerospace stunt earns a quantified 4B baht sales lift, but it functions more as a PR stunt than a piece of film, with the case board showing little actual cinematography to reward.

one_liner: Real shooter threats, delivered as stand-up.
search_doc:
Real shooter threats, delivered as stand-up.
Mechanisms: comedians-as-messengers, format-bait-and-switch, real-quotes-recontextualised, earned-media-driven-pr
Idea: To highlight the absurdity of treating threats of violence as jokes, we asked comedians to incorporate real threats made by school shooters into their stand-up comedy sets. At first the audiences thought these threats were the set-up for a joke — but it soon became clear they were anything but funny.
Insight: School shooters' warning words sound like dark jokes — so the only way to expose how chilling they are is to deliver them as jokes and let the audience freeze.

People tend not to take these threats seriously — they think the person making them is 'just joking'.

Distributed through earned media and social

inferred.insight: School shooters' warning words sound like dark jokes — so the only way to expose how chilling they are is to deliver them as jokes and let the audience freeze.
inferred.one_liner: Real shooter threats, delivered as stand-up.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Smuggles real school-shooter threats into comedy club sets to detonate the 'just joking' defence, earning 1.2B impressions with $0 media spend and helping prevent at least 16 credible school shootings.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of comedian Wanda Sykes at the microphone against a black backdrop, dual columns of background / idea text on the left and results on the right; press quotes strip across the middle; coloured contributor portrait grid at the bottom with tweet thumbnails beneath.

idea.rationale: Smuggling real shooter threats into stand-up routines weaponises the 'just joking' defence the campaign is trying to dismantle — the audience laughs at a set-up, then freezes when the punchline never comes. The structural bait-and-switch is the message and the medium simultaneously, which is the kind of single-mechanism Idea that travels far in earned media.

strategy.rationale: Sandy Hook Promise is the US gun-violence prevention non-profit founded by families of the 2012 elementary school shooting, whose mission is to stop school shootings by training people to recognise warning signs — so a film about recognising verbal threats is core-mission, not adjacent purpose. The board cites a verifiable load-bearing statistic — '80% of school shooters tell someone about their plans' — and a category fact ('guns are now the leading cause of death for US children since 2022') that grounds the urgency in real public-health data.

execution.rationale: Casting recognisable comedians (Margaret Cho, Roy Wood Jr., Jay Pharoah, David Cross, Wanda Sykes) gives the format-bait its credibility, and shooting in real club settings keeps the threat-reveal viscerally uncomfortable. The campaign rides entirely on earned distribution (zero media spend), so orchestration leans on the talent network rather than a paid amplification layer, which limits reach control.

impact.rationale: 'Prevented at least 16 credible school shootings' is the right business outcome for a violence-prevention NGO — that is the behaviour-change number the organisation exists to move — though the methodology behind 'credible' isn't disclosed on the board. The headline 500M views / 1.2B impressions at $0 media spend are entrant-reported and unaudited, but the breadth of unpaid press (NYT, AdAge, Fox News, AOL) is a credible signal of conversation, and the press logos span the political spectrum which matters for a gun-policy adjacent message.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the format-bait — comedians delivering real shooter threats — is a genuinely original structural device on-brief, with a credible behaviour-change outcome (16 prevented shootings) the NGO exists to produce.

Idea: To help people make sure their memories are properly saved, we launched Missed Calls. A new service that allows people to save their voicemails forever. Missed Calls was launched as a web app, where you access your voicemails and are able to download the ones you'd like as MP3 files. And latest research shows that when we've lost someone we love, their voice can help us in our grieving process. These 65,000 messages are in fact not just emotionless megabytes of sound data on a server, but precious memories to many of Telenor's customers.

extracted.tagline: Orchestrating an iconic tribute to the Sydney Opera House
extracted.context: The Sydney Opera House was built in the face of impossible odds. In 1957, the project was awarded to Jørn Utzon, an unproven Danish architect with an incredible Scandinavian-modernist plan. By the time it was completed, it was 14 years late and 13.5x over budget. It faced protests, political pressure, doubters and naysayers — and now stands perhaps the most famous building in the world.
extracted.idea: On its 50th anniversary, celebrate the Opera House as a reminder of the power of brave creativity by creating an original song and music video celebrating the building, with Tim Minchin, Kim Gehrig and other talented artists.

inferred.insight: The Sydney Opera House only exists because someone refused to play it safe — the building itself is a monument to brave creativity worth defending in a risk-averse age.
inferred.one_liner: The Opera House sings its own anthem against playing it safe.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A 50th-anniversary musical film turning the Opera House into a global argument for creative risk, reaching 1.9 billion and triggering 664 international stories.

visual.style_description: Two-panel cinematic film board: left panel shows a regimented row of men in identical brown suits against the Opera House's vertical wooden ribs; right panel cuts to a chaotic, joyful crowd of colourful dancers and singers on the Opera House steps under the famous sails. Layout overlays the campaign title 'Play it Safe' (with 'Safe' inverted) and four large metric numbers over the imagery.

idea.rationale: Turning the Opera House's own troubled origin story into a sung argument for creative bravery is a rare alignment of medium, brand and message — the building is at once subject, stage and metaphor. The 'Play It Safe' titlecard with 'Safe' inverted concentrates the campaign's contrarian thesis into a single typographic gesture, giving the film an idea you can read at a glance.

strategy.rationale: The Sydney Opera House is Australia's most recognised cultural institution and a UNESCO-listed performing-arts venue whose 50th anniversary is the natural moment to renew its public mandate, so a film about creative risk-taking is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The brand's own well-documented history — Utzon, 14 years late, 13.5x over budget, protests and political pressure — is the verifiable insight powering the spot, so the strategic claim rests on cited fact rather than assertion.

execution.rationale: Kim Gehrig directs an original Tim Minchin-written anthem performed by hundreds of Australian singers, dancers and musicians, with the two-panel grammar (regimented brown suits vs. chaotic colourful chorus on the Opera House steps) carrying the brave/safe argument visually. Distribution rides the Opera House's owned channels and earned international press rather than a paid blitz, which is correct for an institution speaking from cultural authority.

impact.rationale: The 1.9 billion reach figure is entrant-reported and unaudited, and the four headline numbers (231 brown suits, 840 tracks, 1.9B reach, 664 international stories) tilt toward production scale and earned conversation rather than a hard business outcome — defensible for an anniversary brand-equity film where the objective is cultural relevance rather than ticket sales. The breadth of 664 international stories is a credible directional proxy for global resonance.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because the Opera House's own origin myth is converted into a sung argument for creative bravery, making the building both subject and proof of the campaign's thesis.

strategy.rationale: EVOKA Foundation is a London-based non-profit advocating for women's freedom in authoritarian contexts, here reviving global attention to the Women, Life, Freedom (Zan, Zendegi, Azadi) movement after the death of Mahsa Jina Amini in September 2022. The context is verifiable — '20,000+ arrested', the morality-police killing, March 2023 attention drop — and mission-fit is exact, but the board treats the post-Amini attention-decay as self-evident rather than citing news-volume data.

execution.rationale: Craft is high: cinematic colour palette, refugee-led casting and protected-identity authorship give the short the moral authority it needs to carry the message. Distribution stacked an outside-Foreign-Office screening with opinion-leader open-letter, a VOA livestream, organic social distribution by European and Middle-Eastern influencers, and pickup by Variety and Manoto TV, which is the right earned-media stack for a purpose film.

impact.rationale: The viewership numbers — '1.33M viewers in America & Manoto TV', '40M+ viewers across Europe & Middle East', 'Variety 28.3M unique users per month' — mix audience-to-film, channel-reach and platform-reach in ways that inflate the apparent scale (Variety's site UVs are not viewers of the film). 'First Oscar & BAFTA short by an anonymous director' is a credentialing claim from Brit Flix rather than an audited outcome, and the real business outcome for a women's-freedom foundation — petition signatures, policy traction, sustained search volume on the movement — is not numerically shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the anonymous-Iranian-women authorship gives the film an unusually credible moral spine, but the impact case mixes reach figures of different kinds rather than demonstrating sustained re-engagement with the movement.

execution: Campaign first unveiled on Twitter, the most engaging platform for football fans
Influencer-posted content in two parts: the first posted as the so-called unseen compilation of French soccer team highlights that excited football fans
Trick revealed by releasing the full video exposing the viewers to their own gender bias
Successful prank quickly gained media attention in France and worldwide within a day of the initial tweet
press_coverage: TIME — "Women's soccer can be just as exciting as men's"

visual.style_description: Hero ensemble portrait of the French women's national team in blue kits against a dark stadium backdrop, with the orange WoMen's Football wordmark anchoring the top; metrics tower and press quotes stack on the right, and a clip strip of swap-reveal stills runs along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Cutting together a 'compilation of unseen highlights of the French men's team' that is in fact the women's team — and only revealing the swap mid-watch — turns the viewer's own admiration into evidence of their bias. The reveal is built into the edit itself, which is the kind of single-stroke film idea that defines a Film Grand Prix.

strategy.rationale: Orange is France's incumbent telco and the long-running official partner of the French national football teams, so positioning itself as the broadcaster-funded amplifier of women's football is on-mission, not borrowed purpose. The context is verifiable and time-pegged — France's 5th Women's World Cup in July 2023 with no broadcaster secured weeks before kick-off — and the insight that French fans hold gender bias against women's football is plausible in a market with documented 'woman bashing' around the sport, though the board states it as fact rather than citing study data.

execution.rationale: The VFX-grade player swap is invisible enough that football fans share the clip enthusiastically before the trick is revealed, which is the entire craft proposition of the film. The release was staged as a two-step social drop on Twitter (the platform most concentrated with male football fans), with a celebrity-influencer first wave posting the 'compilation' and a second wave releasing the full reveal, letting earned PR carry the film into 91 countries within a day.

impact.rationale: Reach numbers are entrant-reported (200M+ organic views, 50M+ likes, 2B+ impressions, 91-country PR pickup), and the percentages have no absolute base, but the breadth of unpaid coverage across TIME and ESPN is a credible signal of cultural conversation. The real business outcome for Orange — broadcast rights for the Women's World Cup eventually secured in France — is not numerically shown on the board, only implied via the campaign's framing, which keeps the case at 'cultural lift' rather than 'measured behaviour change'.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because the film's craft and its idea are the same gesture — an invisible player-swap that turns fans' own delight into proof of their bias before they can look away.
